era_context: Mid-14th century: The Hundred Years' War continues between England and France. The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia under Orhan I and his successor Murad I. China is in the final decades of Yuan Dynasty rule, with Zhu Yuanzhang's forces rising. The Timurid Empire is consolidating in Central Asia. Europe is recovering from the Black Death, experiencing labour shortages and social upheaval.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and South America (limited documentary evidence)
Eastern Europe and Russia (sparse sources for this specific year)

events.title: Zhu Yuanzhang Defeats Chen Youliang at the Battle of Poyang Lake
events.description: Zhu Yuanzhang's forces defeated Chen Youliang, a major rival warlord, at the Battle of Poyang Lake. This was one of the largest naval battles of the medieval world and a decisive victory for Zhu Yuanzhang. The defeat of Chen Youliang eliminated the last major rival to Zhu Yuanzhang's control of southern China and paved the way for his eventual conquest of the north and the founding of the Ming Dynasty.
key_figures: Chen Youliang (rival warlord, defeated and killed)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Poyang Lake is well-attested in Chinese sources. The date 1363 is sometimes cited, but 1361 is the more widely accepted date in modern scholarship.

events.title: Plague Returns to Europe (Second Wave)
events.description: A second major wave of plague (bubonic plague) swept through Europe in 1361–1362, killing hundreds of thousands. This was part of the recurring plague epidemics that would continue throughout the 14th and 15th centuries. The 1361 plague was particularly severe in England and France, exacerbating the labour shortages and social disruptions already caused by the Black Death (1347–1353). The plague's recurrence reinforced medieval anxieties about mortality and divine punishment.
key_figures: Various physicians and clergy (names largely unrecorded)
events.certainty_note: The recurrence of plague in 1361–1362 is well-documented in multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources.

events.title: Edward III Renews Claims to French Throne
events.description: King Edward III of England renewed his claims to the French throne and launched new military campaigns in the Hundred Years' War. In 1361, Edward III was still actively pursuing his dynastic claims, though the war had become increasingly costly and inconclusive. The renewal of claims in 1361 reflected the ongoing tensions between England and France that would define the next decades of warfare.
events.certainty_note: Edward III's ongoing claims and military campaigns are well-documented, but specific diplomatic or military actions in 1361 are less precisely dated.
cross_references: Part of broader Hundred Years' War (1337–1453)

events.title: Timur Defeats Hussain Mirza and Consolidates Central Asia
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane) defeated Hussain Mirza, a rival khan, in Central Asia. This victory was part of Timur's systematic consolidation of power in the region. By 1361, Timur had defeated most rival khans and was establishing himself as the dominant military force in Central Asia, laying the foundation for the Timurid Empire.
events.certainty_note: Timur's campaigns in Central Asia are attested in later sources, but precise dating is difficult. The battle with Hussain Mirza is sometimes dated to 1361–1365.
cross_references: Part of Timur's consolidation of Central Asia (1360s–1370s)

events.title: Orhan I Continues Ottoman Expansion in Anatolia
events.description: Orhan I, the Ottoman Sultan, continued his expansion in Anatolia, consolidating Ottoman control over the region. By 1361, the Ottoman Empire had become a significant regional power, controlling much of northwestern Anatolia. Orhan I's reign (1323–1362) was marked by steady territorial expansion and the establishment of Ottoman administrative structures. His death in 1362 would mark the transition to his son Murad I's more aggressive expansion into the Balkans.
events.certainty_note: Orhan I's reign and expansion are well-documented in Ottoman historiography, but specific events in 1361 are less precisely dated.
cross_references: Leads to 1362-001 (Orhan I dies; Murad I succeeds)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Battle of Poyang Lake (1361-001) is sometimes given as 1363 in different sources. Modern scholarship generally favours 1361, but the discrepancy reflects uncertainty in medieval Chinese dating systems. The significance of the plague's recurrence in 1361 (1361-002) is sometimes minimized in older historiography, which focused on the Black Death of 1347–1353. Modern scholarship has revealed that recurring plague epidemics were equally significant in shaping medieval society. Edward III's specific actions in 1361 (1361-003) are difficult to pinpoint; the broader pattern of his claims and campaigns is clear, but individual events are less precisely dated.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1361 is uneven. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are comprehensive and well-dated for major military events like the Battle of Poyang Lake. European sources (Froissart, Jean de Venette) are reasonably detailed for political and military events, though not always contemporary. Ottoman sources are retrospective and less precise on dating. Central Asian sources (Timurid chronicles) are written decades or centuries after the events they describe. The plague's recurrence is well-documented in multiple sources. Social and economic history is underrepresented; the focus is on elite political and military events. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Southeast Asia have minimal documentary evidence for this year.

graph_edges.note: Victory at Poyang Lake leads to Ming Dynasty proclamation
Plague recurrence is part of broader plague epidemics beginning in 1347
Orhan I's reign ends with his death in 1362
